coredata.dc:title: Novel approach towards the synthesis of carbon-based transparent highly effective flame retardant
coredata.dc:creator: Khose, Rahul V.
Pethsangave, Dattatray A.
Wadekar, Pravin H.
Ray, Alok K.
Some, Surajit
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a novel and facile method is proposed to synthesize a highly effective carbon-based water dispersible flame retardant. This is the first report of carbon-based highly potent transparent flame retardant, which keeps the colour of the cloth intact. A functionalized flame retardant graphene quantum dots (FR-GQD) as a carbon-based material was synthesized using graphene oxide and phosphorous source through a hydrothermal treatment. As prepared transparent FR-GQD solution coated cotton cloth was found to maintain cloth's original colour. In a flame test, FR-GQD coated cloth emitted little smoke initially and after that, it failed to catch fire for more than 300 s and maintained its initial shape. Whereas the control cloth caught fire and burnt completely within 15 s. Flame retardant efficiency of the FR-GQD coated cloth was confirmed by detail flame tests such as limiting oxygen index (LOI), exposure to high heat flux (∼50 kW/cm2) and turbulent premixed flame at high temperature (∼1400 °C). The strength of FR-GQD coated cloth was determined using tensile strength test. This technique of synthesis and application of this water dispersed FR material may find a general approach towards the practical and eco-friendly application of non-toxic FR-GQD as transparent flame retardant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon based
Graphene quantum dots
Flame retardant
Transparent and cotton fibre

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption of GQD and FR-GQD (b) XPS Survey spectra of GQD and FR-GQD (c) XPS spectra of GQD, C1s (d) XPS spectra of FR-GQD, C1s (e) XPS spectra of FR-GQD, O1s (f) XPS spectra of FR-GQD, P2p. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of GQD and FR-GQD, (b) TGA analysis of Blank, GQD coated cloth and FR-GQD coated cloth. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of FR-GQD and GQD coated cloths [a,c] before fire test, respectively. SEM images of FR-GQD and GQD coated cloths [b,d] after fire test, respectively.

Snapshots of the flame treatments of the FR-GQD-coated cloth green (left) and cloth green only (right) with respect to time from (a–d) 0–180 s. Screenshots of the flame retardant test of the GQD coated cloth (left) and blank cloth (right) with respect to time from (e–h) 0–40 s. Screenshots of the flame retardant test of the PPA/NaH2PO4 coated cloth (left) and blank cloth (right) with respect to time from (i–l) 0–40 s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Synthesis of the graphene supported highly potent transparent flame retardant.

head.ce:title: Novel approach towards the synthesis of carbon-based transparent highly effective flame retardant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Dyestuff Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Matunga, Mumbai 400019, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser and Plasma Technology Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a novel and facile method is proposed to synthesize a highly effective carbon-based water dispersible flame retardant. This is the first report of carbon-based highly potent transparent flame retardant, which keeps the colour of the cloth intact. A functionalized flame retardant graphene quantum dots (FR-GQD) as a carbon-based material was synthesized using graphene oxide and phosphorous source through a hydrothermal treatment. As prepared transparent FR-GQD solution coated cotton cloth was found to maintain cloth's original colour. In a flame test, FR-GQD coated cloth emitted little smoke initially and after that, it failed to catch fire for more than 300 s and maintained its initial shape. Whereas the control cloth caught fire and burnt completely within 15 s. Flame retardant efficiency of the FR-GQD coated cloth was confirmed by detail flame tests such as limiting oxygen index (LOI), exposure to high heat flux (∼50 kW/cm2) and turbulent premixed flame at high temperature (∼1400 °C).

The strength of FR-GQD coated cloth was determined using tensile strength test. This technique of synthesis and application of this water dispersed FR material may find a general approach towards the practical and eco-friendly application of non-toxic FR-GQD as transparent flame retardant.

ce:section.ce:para: About 1–2 million years ago human discovered fire and it was biggest and an essential discovery since survival of human race was highly dependent on fire. Till date, fire is an essential part of our day-to-day life. However, as saying goes every advantage has its own shortcoming; fire too has its own disadvantages if not handled with proper care. These fires caused an average of 480 civilian fire deaths, 5540 civilian fire injuries and $1.1 billion in direct property damage [1]. This data is only for the U.S, among which only household fires were considered, the reality is much more horrible if we consider the whole world and different types of fires. This shows that there is a need for a handy flame retardant material which can be used day in and day out such that personal injuries and property losses caused by the fire can be reduced.

Flame retardant textiles with no harmful effects on human body and environment are the general approach to avoid such calamity due to fire. In textile, cotton fabric is one of the excellent materials and is most widely used textile biopolymers in the world to manufacture clothes, home/office decorations like curtains, bed sheets, sofa, bed covers etc. Though it is highly combustible, various approaches have been discovered to improve the combustion characteristics of textiles [2]. The halogenated flame retardants and boron containing materials were extensively used as a fire retardant but such material emits pollutants which can be dangerous to human as well as to the environment and practically can't be used on cloths [3,4].

Phosphorous-containing materials have been extensively used and studied as effective, economic and advantageous flame retardant [5–7]. Phosphorous containing FRs are known for the formation of char residue, which acts as a barrier to extinguish fire [8]. Also, they are resistant to repeated wash cycles which is an extra advantage because they remain more effective than other FRs [9]. To enhance the fire retardation functionality of phosphorous containing materials the most stable and practically usable allotrope of carbon i.e. graphene [10] can be used, but it is difficult to incorporate different atoms in the backbone of the graphene [11]. So many researchers have used derivatives of graphene-like graphene oxide as a starting material for flame retardancy [12–16]. S. Some et al. also had used phosphorous doped graphene oxide as a flame retardant for cotton cloth [17]. D. A. Pethsangave et al. developed an efficient flame retardant for cotton cloth from deep eutectic solvent functionalized graphene composite [18]. H. Liu et al. used phosphorous and nitrogen doped graphene oxide as a flame retardant for cotton cloth [19]. Y. Ji et al. prepared flame retardant silk fabrics coated with reduced graphene oxide [20]. Even though these are efficient flame retardant materials, but practical uses of such materials on cloths are restricted due to masking of the original colour of cloths from these black coloured materials. Thus there is real need to develop a safe, eco-friendly and practically applicable flame retardant (FR) material.

Herein, for the first time, we report the development of a simple procedure to efficiently synthesize flame retardant graphene quantum dot (FR-GQD) to serve as a highly effective carbon-based transparent flame-retardant material. The GQD is prepared by the hydrothermal method in which graphene oxide is used as a starting material [21]. The GQD have some fascinating merits including low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility in water, good biocompatibility and high surface area [22]. This properties of GQD make as an effective material which can be used to prepare transparent flame retardant by the incorporation of phosphorous functional groups on GQD surface. The transparent solution of FR-GQD helps the cloth to retain its original colour.

This is the first report of functionalized graphene quantum dots as carbon-based transparent flame retardant which keeps the colour of the cloth as it is, with efficient protection against high heat flux (∼50 kW/cm2), the turbulent flame at ∼1400 °C and no secondary fire generation. It also doesn't have any harmful effect on human as well as on nature and therefore it will be most applicable flame retardant for day-to-day use.

Graphite powder, potassium permanganate, sulphuric acid, sodium nitrite, hydrogen peroxide, monosodium dihydrogen orthophosphate and poly-phosphoric acid are all purchased from Sigma Aldrich without further purification.
Graphene oxide was prepared from the modified Hummer's method [17].

Graphene oxide was dispersed into water and then PPA and NaH2PO4 were added to it. Above mixture was ultra-sonicated for 30 min and then transferred in a stainless steel autoclave. An Autoclave was kept in the muffle furnace at 150° C for 18 h. After that the solution was filtered off, the filtrate was centrifuged at ∼8000 rpm for 10 min. Then it was evaporated by rotary to get FR-GQD.

The as-prepared FR-GQD materials were characterized by UV–vis Spectroscopy, X-ray Photo electronic Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Thermo-Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). Flame retardant property of FR-GQD solution coated cloth was tested thoroughly by air-ethanol flame test, Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI), exposure to high Heat Flux (∼50 kW/m2) caused by LPG and Turbulent premixed flame test. The flame exposed cloth material was also characterized. To check the endurance of coated cloth, tensile strength test was also carried out.

To explore the optical properties of the GQD and FR-GQD, their UV–vis spectra were recorded (Fig. 1 a). The main UV–vis absorption peak of graphene quantum dots was found to appear at 225 nm which is distinct for GQD and strong background absorption below 300 nm was observed [23]. For the as appeared FR-GQD, absorption maxima was observed at 245 nm, in addition to a peak at 300 nm, which is due to π-π∗ transition. Red shift was observed for the absorption peak of FR-GQD, due to increase in the size of GQD after functionalization [24]. The same trend was observed in case of GO (Fig. S1). Fig. S2 is the photograph of the FR-GQD solution in water under long UV-light. In daylight both GQD and FR-GQD solutions appear as very light yellow in colour and under UV-light greenish blue colour is observed for both of them. Photoluminescence (PL) properties of the GQD and FR-GQD were also studied. PL spectra were recorded in water and the observed peaks are at 437 and 443 nm for GQD and FR-GQD, respectively [Fig. S3]. According to PL data, the red shift was observed in case of FR-GQD in comparison to GQD, due to the functionalization [25].

Prepared materials were analysed by XPS technique to investigate configuration and composition of materials. As-prepared GQD was observed to contain low C/O ratio, whereas FR-GQD has a further lower C/O ratio, which is indicating that FR-GQD contains more oxygen functional groups in comparison to that of GQD (Fig. 1b). In C1s spectra of GQD (Fig. 1c) by XPS, the following peaks were observed at 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.8 eV and 289.3 eV and they are assigned for C-C, C-O, C=O and COOH bonds, respectively [26]. In case of as prepared FR-GQD, C1s spectra (Fig. 1d) have shown the corresponding peaks at 284.6 eV, 287.8 eV and 289.3 eV for C-C, C=O and COOH bonds, respectively. The higher intensity peak of FR-GQD at 286.2 eV comprises of C-O and C-P bonds, indicating phosphorous functionalization. C1s spectra of GO showed respective peaks due to different oxygen functional groups (Fig. S4). In O1s spectra of FR-GQD, it has observed that the peaks at 531.2 eV and 533.0 eV are for C=O, P=O and C-O, C-P, respectively (Fig. 1e). In case of the FR-GQD, the P2p spectra showed the peaks at 133.2 eV and 135.3 eV, which indicates the presence of aromatic C-P, P-O and P=O bonds (Fig. 1f). Thus, it is clearly observed that GQD was well functionalized with the phosphorous functional groups.

The DLS analysis of GQD and FR-GQD showed that the average particles size are up to ∼6 nm and ∼15 nm, respectively, which is due to the functionalization in process [25](Figs. S5(a) and S5(b), respectively).

The FT-IR spectra of GQD and FR-GQD are displayed in (Fig. 2 a). In case of GQD, the peak at 3300 cm−1 indicates the bond vibration of the O-H bond. Peak at 1290 cm−1 was assigned to the hydroxyl functionalization of the GQD and the peak at 1094 cm−1 indicated the presence of the alkoxy [C-O-C]23. The FT-IR spectrum of FR-GQD shows peak at 475 cm−1, indicating the presence of P-O (Rocking), peak at 960 and 1129 cm−1 indicated the presence of P-OH, C-P (Ring) and P=O, respectively. According to FT-IR data, GQD were successfully functionalized with the phosphorous functional groups [10]. The FT-IR spectrum of GO has shown the presence of different oxygen functionalities (Fig. S6).

The TGA was used to evaluate as-prepared material by analysing the weight losses of them. It displays the weight loss of the three samples; pristine cloth, GQD coated cloth and FR-GQD coated cloth (Fig. 2b). Each cotton cloth sample was heated under an air atmosphere from RT to 700 °C at a heating rate of 20° C/min. Both the pristine and GQD coated cloths showed a low thermal stability in an air atmosphere as shown in curves. The major weight loss was observed between 250 and 350 °C, this may due to the release of CO2 and water vapour from the most labile functional groups during the heating process. At a temperature of around 700 °C, the weight loss was more than 80%, which indicated the decomposition of the carbon framework [27]. In contrast to above result, thermal analysis of FR-GQD showed stepwise weight loss at 150–220 °C and total weight loss at 700 °C is 50% only which shows that FR-GQD coated cloth is much more stable than both of blank and GQD coated cloth.

The SEM was used to study surface morphology of as-prepared materials. In case of FR-GQD coated cloth, we have observed the distinct morphology in comparison to GQD. SEM images of FR-GQD coated cloth, before and after flame retarding test, were shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b. Before the flame test, images of the FR-GQD show the presence of the functionalized GQD on cloth surface (Fig. 3a). After the flame test, FR-GQD coated cloth was found to preserve the fiber (Fig. S7) and structure by forming a char residue layer on cloth surface which protects the fiber beneath (Fig. 3b). The fire test was also carried on to the GQD coated cloth. Before the fire test, GQD coated cloth exhibited distinct morphology of GQD on the surface of the fabric (Fig. 3c) and after the fire test, the fabric was found to destroy completely (Fig. 3d). Elemental mapping was performed to exhibit the uniform distribution of phosphorous atom on the FR-GQD surface, which is illustrated in Fig. S8; carbon (grey), oxygen (red) and phosphorous (green).

During exposure to high heat flux (∼50 kW/m2) LPG flame to cloths, heat flux data were collected using calibrated heat sensors (Figs. S13-S17 and Table S4-S5). The turbulent premixed flame test was also performed; temperature of flame for the test was ∼1400 °C (Fig. S18). The tensile strength of the blank, GQD coated cloth and FR-GQD coated cloth have shown that it is reducing after 3 times of coating of FR-GQD (Fig. S19) [30]. Limiting oxygen test have performed by using method ASTM D2863-09 (Table S6) [18], [30]. Wash fastness test of the fabric coated with the material with and without binder was carried out (Table S7-S8) [30].

In conclusion, we have developed a novel and easy method to prepare FR-GQD as an effective transparent carbon-based flame retardant material. This carbon-based transparent highly potent flame retardant helps to keep the colour of the cloth intact. Various phosphorous sources were successfully utilized for the functionalization of GO such as monosodium dihydrogen orthophosphate and poly-phosphoric acid. The as-prepared FR-GQD coated cloth shows high durability in the flame-retardant experiment. When as-prepared FR-GQD is applied to the cotton cloth, it doesn't change or alter the texture and colour of the cloth. In the initial stage of an experiment, FR-GQD coated cloth emitted little smoke and colour of the cloth turned black but after that it didn't catch fire for more than >300 s (5 min) and has maintained its primary form with slight tilting, whereas normal cloth caught fire within 5s and completely burnt out within 15s by producing thin black mass. Excellent flame retardant property and thus no secondary fire generation property of FR-GQD coated cloth is confirmed by performing heat flux test, LOI test and turbulent premixed flame test. FR-GQD coated cloth has shown excellent flame retardation properties, which will be applicable in day to day life. FR-GQD is the first transparent graphene supported nanomaterial could be considered as a general approach toward, eco-friendly, mass production for many practical applications, including flame retardation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by DAE-BRNS research grant fund (34/14/14/2015/BRNS), DST fund (YSS/2015/000788) and UGC Start-Up- Grant (No.F.4-5/2006 (BSR)). R.V.K is thankful to DAE-BRNS for providing research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.06.049.
